title.zh: 卷四十下 班彪列傳
title.raw: 卷四十下 班彪列傳 Volume 40b: Biography of Ban Biao 2

translations.literal: Ban Gu, Part Two.
translations.idiomatic: Ban Gu, Part Two.

sentences.zh: 主人喟然而歎曰：「痛乎風俗之移人也！

translations.literal: The host sighed deeply and said, "How painful that customs shift people!
translations.idiomatic: The host drew a long sigh and said, "How deeply custom remoulds the human heart!

sentences.zh: 子實秦人，鄉誇館室，保界河山，信識昭襄而知始皇矣，惡睹大漢之雲為乎？

translations.literal: You are truly a man of Qin: you have long boasted of palaces and halls, held fast to rivers and mountains as your boundary, and truly know King Zhaoxiang and thus know the First Emperor — how could you have seen what the great Han has achieved in cloud and deed?
translations.idiomatic: You are a son of Qin at heart: you have always prided yourself on palace architecture, on guarding the realm behind river and mountain, and you know King Zhao and King Xiang well enough to know the First Emperor too — so how could you ever have grasped the sweep and substance of the great Han?

sentences.zh: [一]夫大漢之開原也，奮布衣以登皇極，繇數開而創萬世，蓋六籍所不能談，前聖靡得而言焉。

translations.literal: [One] As for the Han's opening of its source: it rose from common cloth to ascend the supreme throne, passed through several "openings" and founded ten thousand generations — matters the Six Classics cannot exhaust, and former sages could not fully speak of.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Consider how the Han opened its rise: from plain cloth to the throne, through successive stages of renewal it laid foundations meant to last ages — a story the Six Classics barely contain and the ancient sages could not wholly put into words.

sentences.zh: [二]當此之時，功有橫而當天，討有逆而順人，故婁敬度埶而獻其說，蕭公權宜以拓其制。

translations.literal: [Two] At that time, merit appeared contrary yet matched Heaven; punitive action was "against the grain" yet accorded with the people; hence Lou Jing gauged the situation and offered his counsel, and Lord Xiao [He] acted as expedient to extend the institutions.
translations.idiomatic: [2] In that age, deeds that seemed to run counter to fate were nonetheless Heaven's match; campaigns that broke the usual order still won the people's assent — which is why Lou Jing read the times and laid his plan before the throne, and Xiao He stretched the system as circumstance required.

translations.literal: Could the times have been utterly peaceful and thus settled?
translations.idiomatic: Surely the age was not one of untroubled ease.

translations.literal: The reckoning could not but be thus.
translations.idiomatic: Necessity, not comfort, dictated every choice.

sentences.zh: [三]吾子曾不是睹，顧耀後嗣之末造，不亦闇乎？

translations.literal: [Three] My good sir, you have never beheld this, yet you turn to glorify the last productions of later heirs — is that not benighted?
translations.idiomatic: [3] Yet you, sir, have never really looked at that, and instead you dwell on the late extravagances of later reigns — is that not blind?

sentences.zh: [四] 今將語子以建武之理，永平之事，監乎*

translations.literal: [Four] Now I shall tell you of the principles of the Jianwu era and the affairs of the Yongping reign, taking as mirror *
translations.idiomatic: [4] I shall now set before you the logic of the Jianwu years and the record of the Yongping reign, holding them up as a mirror to *

translations.literal: (Tai)
translations.idiomatic: (Tai)

translations.literal: **[Great]* clarity, in order to alter your perhaps-fixed intent."
translations.idiomatic: **supreme* clarity, and so reshape whatever fixed ideas you may hold."

translations.literal: [Five] Note [one]: Kui is the appearance of sighing.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Commentary [1]: "Sighed" (kui) describes the manner of sighing.

sentences.zh: 前書曰：「人有剛柔緩急，音聲不同，系水土之風氣，謂之風；

translations.literal: The Former Documents says: "People have hard and soft, slow and quick [temperaments]; their voices differ — tied to the wind-and-air of water and soil, this is called feng (customs);
translations.idiomatic: The Han shu says: "Whether nature runs firm or yielding, urgent or slow, and voices differ with it — because these traits bind to the climate and soil of a place, that is called feng (local custom);

sentences.zh: 好惡取捨，動靜無常，隨君上之情慾，謂之俗。

translations.literal: likes and dislikes, taking and leaving, movement and stillness without constancy, following the ruler's passions and desires — this is called su (popular usage).
translations.idiomatic: While shifting likes and dislikes, what people adopt or reject, motion and rest without a fixed rule, tracking the passions of those above — that is called su, the temper of the age.

sentences.zh: 」保，守也，謂守河山之險以為界。

translations.literal: " Bao means guard — it means guarding the peril of rivers and mountains to make a boundary.
translations.idiomatic: " "Guard" (bao) means to hold fast — here, holding the natural barriers of river and hill as one's frontier.

translations.literal: Zhao and Xiang are King Zhaoxiang and King Xiang.
translations.idiomatic: "Zhao" and "Xiang" refer to King Zhaoxiang and King Xiang of Qin.

translations.literal: Wu means "how" in rhetorical questions; it is read with the same sound as wu "crow."
translations.idiomatic: Here wu means "how" in "how could"; read it with the wu that means "crow," not the wu that means "hate."

sentences.zh: 注[二]漢高祖曰：「吾以布衣，提三尺□取天下。

translations.literal: Note [two]: Emperor Gaozu of Han said: "I, in common cloth, took a three-foot sword to seize the empire."
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: Han Gaozu said: "As a commoner I raised a three-foot sword and won the realm."

sentences.zh: 」高祖起兵五年而即帝位，故雲由數開。

translations.literal: " Gaozu raised troops and in five years ascended the imperial seat; hence it is said he passed through several "openings."
translations.idiomatic: " He went from rebellion to the throne in five years, which is why the text speaks of successive "openings" (turnings).

translations.literal: You here is the same word as you meaning "from" or "by way of."
translations.idiomatic: The graph you here is the same as you meaning "through" or "by way of."

sentences.zh: 孔安國注尚書云：「匝四時曰開。

translations.literal: Kong Anguo's commentary on the Documents says: "A full cycle of the four seasons is called kai (opening).
translations.idiomatic: Kong Anguo's gloss on the Shang shu reads: "When the four seasons have run their round, that is called kai."

translations.literal: " "Ten thousand generations" is said for emphasis.
translations.idiomatic: " "Ten thousand generations" is hyperbole for a lasting foundation.

translations.literal: The Six Ji are the Six Classics.
translations.idiomatic: "The Six Ji" means the Six Classics.

translations.literal: Note [three]: Heng is glossed with the fanqie spelling hu-meng.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: for heng, the fanqie is hu-meng.

sentences.zh: 高祖入關，秦王子嬰降，而五星聚於東井，此功有橫而當天也。

translations.literal: When Gaozu entered the Pass, Prince Ziying of Qin surrendered, and the Five Planets gathered at the Eastern Well — this is merit appearing contrary yet matching Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: When the High Ancestor entered the passes, the Qin heir Ziying submitted and the five planets clustered in Dongjing — that is the "contrary" deed that nonetheless aligned with Heaven.

translations.literal: Ni means using a subject to attack the ruler.
translations.idiomatic: Ni ("against the grain") means a minister striking at his lord.

sentences.zh: 前書陸賈曰：「湯武逆取而以順守之。

translations.literal: The Former Documents records Lu Jia as saying: "Tang and Wu took by reversal yet guarded by accordance."
translations.idiomatic: The Han shu quotes Lu Jia: "Tang and Wu seized power in a way that broke the old order, but they kept it by ruling with the people's consent."

sentences.zh: 」及高祖入關，秦人爭獻牛酒，此為討有逆而順人也。

translations.literal: " And when Gaozu entered the Pass, the people of Qin vied to present oxen and wine — this is punitive action being "against the grain" yet according with the people.
translations.idiomatic: " When Gaozu entered the passes, Qin commoners rushed forward with cattle and wine — that is the "punitive" campaign that still won the people.

translations.literal: Lou Jing has already appeared above.
translations.idiomatic: Lou Jing has been treated earlier in the text.

sentences.zh: 又曰：「蕭何修未央宮，上見壯麗，甚怒。

translations.literal: It also says: "Xiao He built Weiyang Palace; when the emperor saw its grandeur he was greatly angry.
translations.idiomatic: The same tradition says: "When Xiao He built Weiyang Palace and the sovereign saw how grand it was, he flared with anger."

sentences.zh: 何對曰：『天下未定，故可因遂就宮室。

translations.literal: He replied: 'The realm is not yet settled, so we may take the occasion to complete the palaces.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao He answered: "The empire is still unsettled, so we may use the moment to finish the palaces."

sentences.zh: 且天子以四海為家，非令壯麗，無以重威，且無令後代有以加也。

translations.literal: Moreover the Son of Heaven takes the four seas as his house; unless it is made magnificent, there is no way to lend weight to awe, and none for later ages to surpass."'
translations.idiomatic: Besides, the Son of Heaven holds all under heaven as one household; if the halls were not imposing, they could not lend majesty to authority, nor keep posterity from trying to outdo them."

translations.literal: "'" Could the times have been extravagant and peaceful and thus settled?
translations.idiomatic: " Was that an age of lavish ease? Hardly.

sentences.zh: 言天下初定，計不得止而都西京也。

translations.literal: It means that when the realm was first settled, the reckoning could not stop at making the western capital.
translations.idiomatic: It means the empire had just been pacified and necessity drove the choice of the western capital — there was no stopping short of that.

translations.literal: Note [four]: Gu means "on the contrary" or "instead."
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: gu means turning back or "on the contrary."

translations.literal: Yao means to display or show off.
translations.idiomatic: Yao means to flaunt or put on display.

sentences.zh: 言吾子曾不睹度埶權宜之由，而反炫耀後嗣子孫末代之所造，非其盛稱武帝、成帝神仙、昭陽之事也。

translations.literal: It means that my good sir has never beheld the reasons for gauging situation and expedient, but on the contrary displays the last productions of later heirs and descendants — is it not precisely vaunting Emperor Wu and Emperor Cheng on immortals and the Zhaoyang affair?
translations.idiomatic: That is: you have not weighed how policy bent to circumstance, yet you parade the late excesses of later generations — the very boasting about Emperor Wu's and Emperor Cheng's immortality cults and the Zhaoyang palace scandals.

sentences.zh: 注[五]淮南子曰：「太清之化也，和順以寂漠，質直以素樸。

translations.literal: Note [five]: The Huainanzi says: "The transformation of Supreme Clarity: harmony and compliance with still silence, plainness with simplicity."
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [5]: The Huainanzi says: "Under Great Purity's transforming influence, things move in harmony and quiet, in plain simplicity."

translations.literal: " Gao You's commentary says: "Supreme Clarity is the transformation of non-action."
translations.idiomatic: " Gao You glosses: "Supreme Clarity is government by non-action."

sentences.zh: 往者王莽作逆，漢祚中缺，天人致誅，六合相滅。

translations.literal: In former times Wang Mang rebelled against the Han; the Han mandate was broken in the middle; Heaven and man together inflicted punishment; the six directions destroyed one another.
translations.idiomatic: Once Wang Mang usurped the Han, the dynasty's mandate snapped midway; Heaven and the human world joined in retribution, and the six quarters consumed one another.

sentences.zh: [一]於時之亂，生民幾亡，鬼神泯絕，壑無完柩，郛罔遺室，原野猒人之肉，川谷流人之血，秦、項之災猶不克半，書契已來未之或紀也。

translations.literal: [One] In the disorder of that time, the living people nearly perished, ghosts and spirits were extinguished, gullies held no intact coffins, outer walls left no surviving chambers, the open fields were sated with human flesh, streams and valleys flowed with human blood — the disasters of Qin and Xiang did not reach half of it; since writing began, none has recorded the like.
translations.idiomatic: [1] In that chaos the people almost vanished, the spirits seemed cut off, ravines held no whole coffin, not a house stood within the walls, the plains glutted themselves on human flesh and the rivers ran with human gore — worse by half than anything Qin or Xiang Yu had wrought; since records began, nothing like it had been set down.

sentences.zh: [二]故下民號而上愬，上帝懷而降鑒，致命於聖皇。

translations.literal: [Two] Hence the lower people cried out and the upper complained; the High Lord took pity and looked down — and entrusted the mandate to the sage emperor.
translations.idiomatic: [2] So the common folk cried aloud to those above, and High Heaven, moved to pity, bent a watching eye and handed the charge to the sage sovereign.

sentences.zh: [三]於是聖皇乃握干符，闡坤珍，披皇圖，稽帝文，赫爾發憤，應若興雲，霆發昆陽，憑怒雷震。

translations.literal: [Three] Thereupon the sage emperor grasped the stem talisman, unfolded the treasures of earth, spread the imperial charts, examined the emperor's pattern-text; fiercely he roused his wrath, responding like rising clouds; lightning burst at Kunyang, his fury mounted like thunder.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Then that sage ruler took up Heaven's tally, disclosed earth's prodigies, unrolled the imperial charts, scanned the prophetic writings; in blazing anger he rose like gathering clouds, his thunderstroke fell at Kunyang, his wrath rolled like peals of thunder.

sentences.zh: [四]遂超大河，跨北嶽，立號高邑，建都河洛。

translations.literal: [Four] Thus he crossed the great river, strode the northern peak, established his title at Gaoyi, and founded the capital at the Luo River.
translations.idiomatic: [4] He crossed the Yellow River, stood astride the Northern Marchmount, proclaimed his reign at Gaoyi, and fixed his capital on the Luo.

sentences.zh: [五] 紹百王之荒屯，因造化之蕩滌，體元立制，繼天而作。

translations.literal: [Five] He continued the hundred kings' wasteland hardship, rode the great wash of creation's cleansing, embodied the origin and set institutions, succeeded Heaven and undertook the work.
translations.idiomatic: [5] He took up the tangled legacy of a hundred kings, passed through creation's sweeping purge, anchored policy in the primal pattern, and acted as Heaven's successor in founding anew.

sentences.zh: [六]系唐統，接漢緒，茂育腢生，恢復疆宇，動兼乎在昔，事勤乎三五。

translations.literal: [Six] He tied his line to Tang's succession and took up Han's thread, richly nurtured the myriad living, restored the borders and domain; his deeds embraced the ancients, his labors matched those of the Three August Ones and Five Emperors.
translations.idiomatic: [6] He linked his house to Tang's line and resumed Han's thread, brought the myriad creatures to flourish again, and won back land and sway; his achievements match the old exemplars, his toil equals that of the Three Sovereigns and Five Thearchs.

sentences.zh: [七]豈特方軌並多，紛綸後辟，理近古之所務，蹈一聖之險易云爾哉？

translations.literal: [Seven] How could it be only that he ran in the same rut with many peers, that later rulers jostled in confusion, that he merely did what recent antiquity pursued, or trod one sage's easy or perilous path — so to speak?
translations.idiomatic: [7] Surely he was not merely keeping pace with a crowd of rulers, or repeating the muddled efforts of later lords, or doing only what near antiquity attempted, or retracing a single sage's smooth or steep road?

sentences.zh: [八]且夫建武之元，天地革命，四海之內，更造夫婦，□有父子，君臣初建，人倫寔始，斯乃虙羲氏之所以基皇德也。

translations.literal: [Eight] Moreover, at the first year of Jianwu, Heaven and earth underwent revolution; within the four seas, husbands and wives were remade anew, and there were again fathers and sons; ruler and minister were first established, human relations truly began — this is how Fuxi's kind laid the base of imperial virtue.
translations.idiomatic: [8] At the opening of Jianwu, Heaven and earth turned over; within the four seas families were forged anew, fathers and sons recognized anew; lord and minister took shape for the first time, the human order truly began — the very way Fuxi's line grounded royal virtue.

sentences.zh: [九]分州土，立市朝，作舟車，造器械，斯軒轅氏之所以開帝功也。

translations.literal: [Nine] He divided the provinces' soil, set up markets and courts, made boats and carts, fashioned tools and weapons — this is how the Xuanyuan kind opened imperial achievement.
translations.idiomatic: [9] He apportioned the land into provinces, founded market and court, built boats and carts, forged tools and arms — the way the Yellow Thearch's line opened imperial merit.

translations.literal: [Ten] He reverently carried out Heaven's punishment, responding to Heaven and following *
translations.idiomatic: [10] He solemnly executed Heaven's sentence, answering Heaven and following *

translations.literal: (the people)
translations.idiomatic: (the people)

sentences.zh: **[人]*，斯乃湯武之所以昭王業也。

translations.literal: **[men]* — this is how Tang and Wu displayed kingly enterprise.
translations.idiomatic: **the people* — the way Tang and Wu made kingship shine.

sentences.zh: [一一]遷都改邑，有殷宗中興之則焉；

translations.literal: [Eleven] Moving the capital and changing the city has the model of the Shang house's restoration;
translations.idiomatic: [11] Shifting the capital and seat of rule follows the precedent of the Shang restoration;

sentences.zh: 即土之中，有周成隆平之制焉。

translations.literal: occupying the center of the land has the system of Zhou's Cheng and his prosperous peace.
translations.idiomatic: occupying the central ground repeats the order of King Cheng's peaceful height.

sentences.zh: [一二]不階尺土一人之柄，同符乎高祖。

translations.literal: [Twelve] Without relying on an inch of soil or the handle of a single other man, he matched the tallies of the High Ancestor.
translations.idiomatic: [12] Without inheriting an inch of territory or another man's authority, he matched Gaozu's celestial signs.

sentences.zh: [一三]克己復禮，以奉終始，允恭乎孝文。

translations.literal: [Thirteen] Restraining self and returning to ritual, serving from first to last with perfect reverence — he matched Emperor Xiaowen.
translations.idiomatic: [13] In curbing desire and restoring the rites, in dutiful care from beginning to end, he rivaled Emperor Wen.

sentences.zh: [一四]憲章稽古，封岱勒成，儀炳乎世宗。

translations.literal: [Fourteen] Taking statutes from antiquity, enfeoffing Mount Dai and carving the completion, his rites shone like the Shizong.
translations.idiomatic: [14] In modeling himself on the past, in the Mount Tai fief and the inscribed triumph, his ceremonial splendor matched Emperor Wu.

sentences.zh: [一五]案六經而校德，妙古昔而論功，仁聖之事既該，帝王之道備矣。

translations.literal: [Fifteen] Examining the Six Classics to compare virtue, weighing antiquity to discuss merit — the deeds of benevolence and sagehood are complete, the way of emperors and kings is fully provided.
translations.idiomatic: [15] When he tested his virtue against the Six Classics and weighed his merit against the ages, the humane and sagely enterprise stood whole, the path of emperor and king complete.

sentences.zh: [一六] 注[一]天人謂天意人事共相誅也。

translations.literal: [Sixteen] Note [one]: "Heaven and man" means Heaven's intent and human affairs jointly inflicted punishment.
translations.idiomatic: [16] Commentary [1]: "Heaven and man" means divine will and human action joined in retribution.

translations.literal: Note [two]: Man is the master of the spirits.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: "The living are the lords of the spirits."

sentences.zh: 生人既亡，故鬼神亦絕也。

translations.literal: When the living people had perished, therefore ghosts and spirits were also cut off.
translations.idiomatic: With mankind nearly gone, the spirits too seemed to vanish.

sentences.zh: 揚子法言曰「秦將白起長平之戰，坑四十萬人，原野猒人之肉，川谷流人之血」也。

translations.literal: Master Yang's Model Sayings says: "When Qin general Bai Qi at the battle of Changping buried four hundred thousand men, the open fields were sated with human flesh, streams and valleys flowed with human blood."
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xiong's Fa yan says: "At Bai Qi's Changping massacre of four hundred thousand, the plains fed on corpses and the valleys ran with blood."

translations.literal: Note [three]: Shangdi is Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Shangdi means Heaven.

translations.literal: The sage emperor is Guangwu.
translations.idiomatic: "The sage sovereign" is Guangwu (Emperor Guang).

translations.literal: Huai means to pity and remember.
translations.idiomatic: Huai here means to cherish and pity.

translations.literal: Jiang means to descend.
translations.idiomatic: Jiang means to come down or descend.

translations.literal: Jian means to look upon.
translations.idiomatic: Jian means to survey or look down upon.

sentences.zh: 言上天□念下人之上愬，故下視四海可以為君者，而致命於光武也。

translations.literal: It means that High Heaven took pity and remembered the people's crying complaint above, therefore looked down on the four seas for one who could be ruler, and entrusted the mandate to Guangwu.
translations.idiomatic: That is: High Heaven, moved by the people's upward cry, looked across the realm for a fit ruler and laid the mandate on Guangwu.

sentences.zh: 注[四]干符、坤珍謂天地符瑞也。

translations.literal: Note [four]: Gan fu and kun zhen mean the talismans and prodigies of Heaven and earth.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: "Stem talisman" and "earth's treasures" denote Heaven-and-earth portents.

sentences.zh: 皇圖、帝文謂圖緯之文也。

translations.literal: Huang tu and di wen mean the texts of charts and weft-apocrypha.
translations.idiomatic: "Imperial charts" and "emperor's pattern-text" refer to prognosticatory chart-and-weft literature.

translations.literal: Ting means sudden thunder.
translations.idiomatic: Ting is a sudden clap of thunder.

sentences.zh: 發於昆陽謂破王尋、王邑。

translations.literal: Bursting forth at Kunyang means defeating Wang Xun and Wang Yi.
translations.idiomatic: "Lightning at Kunyang" means the destruction of Wang Xun and Wang Yi.

translations.literal: Ping means abundant or full.
translations.idiomatic: Ping here means swelling to the full or in great force.

translations.literal: It means his abundant wrath was like thunder's shaking.
translations.idiomatic: That is, his wrath rolled with thunder's violence.

translations.literal: For rhyme it is read like zhen.
translations.idiomatic: The reading is adjusted to zhen for the rhyme.

translations.literal: Note [five]: Kua means to straddle or occupy.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [5]: kua means to plant oneself across or hold.

sentences.zh: 言光武度河據北嶽，遂即位於鄗，而改鄗為高邑也。

translations.literal: It means that Guangwu crossed the river and occupied the northern peak, then ascended the throne at Hao and changed Hao to Gaoyi.
translations.idiomatic: That is: Guangwu crossed the river, took his stand on the Northern Marchmount, ascended at Hao, and renamed Hao as Gaoyi.

translations.literal: Note [six]: Shao means to continue.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [6]: shao means to carry on a line.

translations.literal: Tun means hardship or obstruction.
translations.idiomatic: Tun means difficulty or a hard pass.

sentences.zh: 高誘注淮南子云：「造化，天地也。

translations.literal: Gao You's commentary on the Huainanzi says: "Zaohua means Heaven and earth.
translations.idiomatic: Gao You on the Huainanzi: "Zaohua (creation) means Heaven and earth."

translations.literal: Di means to wash away.
translations.idiomatic: Di means to scour clean.

translations.literal: Zuo means to rise or begin.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo means to set something going or begin.

sentences.zh: 杜預注左傳云：「凡人君即位，欲體元以居正。

translations.literal: Du Yu's commentary on the Zuo Tradition says: "Whenever a lord of men first ascends the throne, he wishes to embody the origin and dwell in correctness.
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu on the Zuo zhuan: "When a ruler first takes the throne, he seeks to embody the primal pattern and stand in the right."

translations.literal: " The Guliang Commentary says: "He who is master for all under Heaven is Heaven;
translations.idiomatic: " The Guliang zhuan says: "The true master of the realm is Heaven;

translations.literal: he who succeeds Heaven is the ruler."
translations.idiomatic: he who succeeds Heaven is the sovereign."

translations.literal: Note [seven]: The Erya says: "Xi means to continue;
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [7]: the Erya says that the word glossed here as "tie on" means to continue the line;

translations.literal: xu means enterprise."
translations.idiomatic: and xu means the thread of an undertaking."

translations.literal: " The Former Documents says: "The Han emperor's original line issued from the Tang thearch.
translations.idiomatic: " The Han shu says: "The Han imperial line stems from the Tang thearch;

translations.literal: " It means Guangwu could continue the succession and enterprise of Tang Yao.
translations.idiomatic: " meaning Guangwu could carry on the line and work of Tang Yao.

translations.literal: Hui means great or vast.
translations.idiomatic: Hui means vast or wide-reaching.

translations.literal: San wu means the Three August Ones and Five Emperors.
translations.idiomatic: "Three and five" (san wu) means the Three Sovereigns and Five Thearchs.

translations.literal: Note [eight]: Gui means rut or track.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [8]: gui is the wheel-track, hence a course.

translations.literal: Fenlun is like mixed trampling.
translations.idiomatic: Fenlun suggests a tangled crowd, as if trampled together.

translations.literal: The Erya says: "Hou, pi — jun (ruler).
translations.idiomatic: The Erya: "Hou and pi both mean ruler."

translations.literal: " Xian yi is like li luan (order and disorder).
translations.idiomatic: " "Easy or perilous" (xian yi) parallels order and chaos.

sentences.zh: 言光武功德勤勞，兼於前代百王，非直一聖帝也。

translations.literal: It means Guangwu's merit and toil embraced those of the former age's hundred kings — not merely one sage emperor.
translations.idiomatic: That is, Guangwu's labor and achievement sum those of a hundred earlier kings, not a single sage alone.

sentences.zh: 注[九]易曰：「天地革而四時成。

translations.literal: Note [nine]: The Changes says: "Heaven and earth are transformed and the four seasons complete.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [9]: The Zhou yi: "Heaven and earth revolve in change, and the four seasons complete their course;

translations.literal: " It also says: "Tang and Wu's revolution."
translations.idiomatic: " and again: "The revolution of Tang and Wu."

sentences.zh: 」爾雅曰：「九夷、八狄、七戎、六蠻，謂之四海。

translations.literal: " The Erya says: "The nine Yi, eight Di, seven Rong, and six Man are called the four seas.
translations.idiomatic: " The Erya lists the nine Yi, eight Di, seven Rong, and six Man as "the four seas."

translations.literal: Ji means to begin or found.
translations.idiomatic: Ji means to lay the first stone or begin.

sentences.zh: 帝王紀曰：「庖犧氏，風姓也。

translations.literal: The Di wang ji says: "The Paoxi clan bore the surname Feng.
translations.idiomatic: The royal annals record that the Paoxi people took the surname Feng.

sentences.zh: 制嫁娶之禮，取犧牲以充庖廚，以食天下，故號庖犧。

translations.literal: He instituted the rites of marriage, took sacrificial victims to stock the kitchens, and fed the realm — hence he was called Paoxi.
translations.idiomatic: He fixed marriage rites, drove victims into the royal kitchens to feed all under heaven, and earned the name Paoxi, "he who fills the kitchen."

translations.literal: Later some called him Fuxi.
translations.idiomatic: Later writers also called him Fuxi.

sentences.zh: 」言光武更造夫婦如伏犧時也。

translations.literal: " It means Guangwu remade husbands and wives as in Fuxi's time.
translations.idiomatic: " That is, Guangwu refashioned family life on the model of Fuxi's age.

translations.literal: Note [ten]: The Yellow Emperor's clan was called Xuanyuan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [10]: the Yellow Thearch's house was the Xuanyuan clan.

sentences.zh: 前書曰：「昔在黃帝，畫野分州。

translations.literal: The Former Documents says: "Of old the Yellow Emperor drew the wilds and divided the provinces.
translations.idiomatic: The Han shu says: "In antiquity the Yellow Thearch mapped the wastes and carved the land into provinces."

translations.literal: " The Changes, "Appended Remarks" says: "The Shennong clan held a market at noon.
translations.idiomatic: " The Zhou yi appendices say: "Shennong opened a market at midday."

sentences.zh: 黃帝、堯、舜垂衣裳而天下理。

translations.literal: The Yellow Emperor, Yao, and Shun let robes hang down and the realm was ordered.
translations.idiomatic: The Yellow Thearch, Yao, and Shun needed only to let their robes fall properly and the world fell into order."

sentences.zh: 刳木為舟，剡木為楫，服牛乘馬，引重致遠，以利天下；

translations.literal: They hollowed wood for boats and cut wood for oars; they harnessed oxen and rode horses to haul heavy loads to distant places, to benefit the realm;
translations.idiomatic: They hollowed trunks for boats and shaped oars, yoked oxen and saddled horses to drag heavy freight to far shores, all for the good of the realm;

sentences.zh: 弦木為弧，剡木為矢，弧矢之利，以威天下。

translations.literal: they bent wood for bows and cut wood for arrows; the benefit of bow and arrow was to awe the realm.
translations.idiomatic: they bent wood into bows and cut shafts for arrows, so that the power of the bow could overawe the world."

translations.literal: " It means Guangwu benefited the people as Xuanyuan did.
translations.idiomatic: " In the same way, Guangwu brought profit to the people as the Yellow Thearch had done.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]尚書武王曰：「今予惟龔行天之罰。

translations.literal: Note [eleven]: The Documents, King Wu says: "Now I alone reverently execute Heaven's punishment.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [11]: the Shang shu has King Wu declare: "This day I solemnly carry out Heaven's sentence."

sentences.zh: 」易曰：「湯武革命，順乎天而應乎人。

translations.literal: " The Changes says: "Tang and Wu's revolution accorded with Heaven and responded to the people.
translations.idiomatic: " The Changes adds: "The revolutions of Tang and Wu matched Heaven and answered the people."

translations.literal: " It means Guangwu campaigned as Tang and Wu did.
translations.idiomatic: " That is, Guangwu's wars followed the pattern of Tang and Wu.

sentences.zh: 注[一二]尚書曰：「盤庚遷於殷。

translations.literal: Note [twelve]: The Documents says: "Pan Geng moved to Yin.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [12]: the Documents record Pan Geng's removal to Yin."

sentences.zh: 」史記曰：「帝陽甲之時，殷衰，諸侯莫朝。

translations.literal: " The Records says: "In the time of Emperor Yang Jia, Yin declined and the feudal lords ceased their court visits.
translations.idiomatic: " The Shi ji notes that under Emperor Yang Jia Yin weakened until the lords no longer came to court."

sentences.zh: 陽甲崩，弟盤庚立，自河北度河南，居湯之故地，行湯之政，殷道復興。

translations.literal: When Yang Jia died his younger brother Pan Geng succeeded; from north of the river he crossed to south of the river, dwelt in Tang's old territory, practiced Tang's government, and the Yin way revived.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Jia died and his younger brother Pan Geng took the throne; he crossed from the north to the south bank of the river, settled in Tang's ancient seat, revived Tang's policies, and the house of Yin flourished again."

sentences.zh: 」尚書曰：「王來紹上帝，自服於土中。

translations.literal: " The Documents says: "The king came to continue Shangdi and personally took his place in the center of the land.
translations.idiomatic: " The Shang shu says: "The king came to serve High God and took his stand in the middle of the realm."

translations.literal: " Kong Anguo says: "Luoyi is the center of the terrain.
translations.idiomatic: " Kong Anguo explains that Luoyang sits at the geographical midpoint."

sentences.zh: 」春秋命歷序曰：「成康之隆，醴泉湧出。

translations.literal: " The Chunqiu ming li xu says: "At the height of Cheng and Kang, sweet springs welled forth.
translations.idiomatic: " The Chunqiu ming li xu tells us that under Kings Cheng and Kang sweet springs gushed up."

sentences.zh: 」言都洛陽如殷宗、周成之制也。

translations.literal: " It means governing at Luoyang followed the measures of the Yin house's restoration and Zhou's Cheng.
translations.idiomatic: " Thus fixing the capital at Luoyang repeated the Yin restoration and the peaceful order of Zhou's King Cheng.

sentences.zh: 注[一三]孟子曰：「紂去武丁未久也，尺地莫非其有也，一人莫非其臣也。

translations.literal: Note [thirteen]: Mencius says: "Zhou was not long removed from Wu Ding; not an inch of land was not his, not a man was not his subject.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [13]: Mencius observes that the last king of Shang was not many reigns after Wu Ding: every foot of soil was already his, every man already his subject."

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「舜文王相去千有餘歲，若合符契。」

translations.literal: " It also says: "Shun and King Wen were more than a thousand years apart, yet were as if matching tallies."
translations.idiomatic: " He adds that Shun and King Wen stood a millennium apart yet aligned like matching tallies."

sentences.zh: 注[一四]左傳仲尼曰：「古有志，克己復禮，仁也。

translations.literal: Note [fourteen]: The Zuo Tradition records Confucius as saying: "In antiquity there was the maxim: restraining the self and returning to ritual is benevolence.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [14]: Confucius in the Zuo zhuan cites an old saying: to master oneself and return to the rites is humanity."

translations.literal: " Master Xun says: "Birth is man's beginning;
translations.idiomatic: " Xunzi says: "Life is where the human path begins;

translations.literal: death is man's end.
translations.idiomatic: death is where it ends;

translations.literal: When beginning and end are both good, the human way is complete.
translations.idiomatic: when both ends are good, the human way is fulfilled."

translations.literal: " The Documents: "Truly respectful, able to yield.
translations.idiomatic: " The Documents praise "sincere reverence and ready deference."

translations.literal: " It means personally practicing frugality, the same as Emperor Wen.
translations.idiomatic: " That is, ruling from personal austerity like Emperor Wen of Han.

translations.literal: Note [fifteen]: Xianzhang means model or rule.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [15]: xianzhang means a pattern to follow.

translations.literal: The Record of Rites says: "Confucius took Wen and Wu as his models.
translations.idiomatic: The Li ji says Confucius modeled his teaching on King Wen and King Wu."

translations.literal: " The Documents says: "He examined antiquity, Emperor Yao.
translations.idiomatic: " The Shang shu opens: "Examining antiquity, there was Emperor Yao."

sentences.zh: 」言法乎考古而封太山，勒石以記成功也。

translations.literal: " It means modeling on antiquity to enfeoff Mount Tai and inscribe stone to record success.
translations.idiomatic: " That is, taking antiquity as law, fiefing Mount Tai, and carving stone to proclaim the achievement.

sentences.zh: 炳，明也，其禮儀明乎武帝也。

translations.literal: Bing means bright; the ritual and ceremony matched the brightness of Emperor Wu.
translations.idiomatic: Bing means "radiant": the rites shone with the same brilliance as Emperor Wu's reign.

sentences.zh: 注[一六]六經謂詩、書、禮、樂、易、春秋。

translations.literal: Note [sixteen]: The Six Classics mean the Odes, Documents, Rites, Music, Changes, and Spring and Autumn.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [16]: the Six Classics are the Shi, Shu, Li, Yue, Yi, and Chunqiu.

translations.literal: Miao means beautiful.
translations.idiomatic: Miao here means "admirable" or "fine."

translations.literal: Some manuscripts write the graph with the "eye" radical meaning distant.
translations.idiomatic: Some copies use an alternate graph meaning "far-reaching" instead of "wonderful."

translations.literal: Gai means complete.
translations.idiomatic: Gai means comprehensive or all-inclusive.

sentences.zh: 至於永平之際，重熙而累洽，盛三雍之上儀，修袞龍之法服，敷洪藻，信景鑠，揚世廟，正予樂。

translations.literal: By the time of Yongping, splendor was piled on splendor and harmony had accumulated; the high rites of the Three Halls were raised, the lawful robes with coiled dragons were perfected, great literary edicts were spread, the glorious gleam was displayed, imperial shrines were exalted, and the Great Yu music was set right.
translations.idiomatic: Under Yongping, brilliance mounted upon brilliance and concord deepened: the Three Halls rites ascended, the dragon-robe code was perfected, resounding edicts went forth, splendor was proclaimed, dynastic shrines were honored, and the imperial music was brought to order.

sentences.zh: 人神之和允洽，君臣之序既肅。

translations.literal: The accord of men and spirits was truly thorough, the order of lord and minister already solemn.
translations.idiomatic: Men and spirits moved in true harmony; ruler and minister already stood in strict order.

sentences.zh: [一]乃動大路，遵皇衢，省方巡狩，窮覽萬國之有無，考聲教之所被，散皇明以燭幽。

translations.literal: [One] Then he moved the great road, followed the imperial highways, inspected the regions on tour, exhaustively viewing what the myriad states had or lacked, examining where sound and teaching reached, scattering imperial brightness to illumine the hidden.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Then the great highway rolled, the royal thoroughfares were taken, and the sovereign toured the quarters, scanning what every state possessed or lacked, tracing where his voice and teaching had penetrated, and casting imperial light into the dark corners.

sentences.zh: [二]然後增周舊，修洛邑，翩翩巍巍，顯顯翼翼，光漢京於諸夏，總八方而為之極。

translations.literal: [Two] Then he enlarged Zhou's old foundation and repaired Luoyi — soaring, towering, bright, stately — making the Han capital resplendent among the Hua states, summing the eight directions as their center.
translations.idiomatic: [2] He enlarged Zhou's old ground and rebuilt Luoyang until it loomed bright and stately, setting the Han capital above the central states and drawing the eight quarters to one pivot.

sentences.zh: [三]是以皇城之內，宮室光明，闕庭神麗，奢不可踰，儉不能侈。

translations.literal: [Three] Thus within the imperial city the palaces shone, towers and courts were divinely splendid: extravagance could not surpass them, frugality could not belittle them.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Inside the royal precinct the halls blazed, gate-towers and courtyards seemed almost divine — neither wasteful excess nor austere plainness could fault them.

sentences.zh: [四]外則因原野以作苑，順流泉而為沼，發蘋藻以潛魚，豐圃草以毓獸，制同乎梁騶，義合乎靈囿。

translations.literal: [Four] Without, he fashioned parks from the open wilds, made pools by channeling flowing springs, sent forth duckweed and algae for fish to hide in, made the garden grass lush to nurture beasts — institutions matching Liang You's park, righteousness in accord with the sacred preserve.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Beyond the walls he turned the open wild into hunting parks, led living springs into ponds, floated plants for fish to shelter among, fattened the meadow grass to rear game — the same scheme as the royal park of Liang, the same moral intent as the Zhou king's spiritual preserve.

sentences.zh: [五] 若乃順時節而搜狩，簡車徒以講武，則必臨之以王制，考之以風雅。

translations.literal: [Five] When, moreover, he followed the seasons in the great hunts, reviewed chariots and foot soldiers to drill the host, he necessarily brought the royal regulations to bear and tested all by the airs and odes of the states.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Whenever he timed the great seasonal hunts, marshaled chariots and foot to drill the army, he always measured the host against the royal ordinances and weighed conduct with the Classic of Poetry.

sentences.zh: [六]歷騶虞，覽四驖，嘉車攻，采吉日，禮官正儀，乘輿乃出。

translations.literal: [Six] He passed through Zouyu, surveyed Sijian, praised Che gong, gathered the auspicious day; the masters of ceremony set the forms, and then the imperial carriage went forth.
translations.idiomatic: [6] He sang "Zou Yu," studied "Four Bay Steeds," took "Chariot Assault" as his model, chose an auspicious day; the masters of rites aligned the protocol, and only then did the imperial equipage move.

sentences.zh: [七]於是發鯨魚，鏗華鐘，登玉輅，乘時龍，鳳蓋颯灑，和鸞玲瓏，天官景從，祲威盛容。

translations.literal: [Seven] Thereupon the whale-drums sounded, the splendid bells chimed; he mounted the jade chariot, rode the seasonal dragons; phoenix canopies rustled, reassuring bells tinkled; celestial officers followed like shadows, solemn might and splendid bearing.
translations.idiomatic: [7] Whale-hide drums thundered, jeweled bells pealed; he mounted the jade carriage, rode the horses of the seasons; phoenix umbrellas hissed in the wind, harmony bells chimed; starry officers trailed him like shadows, majesty and pomp filled the air.

sentences.zh: [九]山靈護野，屬御方神，雨師泛灑，風伯清塵，千乘雷起，萬騎紛紜，元戎竟野，戈鋋彗雲，羽旄掃霓，旌旗拂天。

translations.literal: [Nine] Mountain spirits protected the wilds, attending deities ranged in square; the rain master sprinkled broadly, the wind lord swept the dust; a thousand chariots rose like thunder, ten thousand riders swarmed; the great war-chariots filled the plain, spear-points rivaled the clouds, feather banners swept the rainbow, pennants brushed the sky.
translations.idiomatic: [9] Genii of the hills guarded the fields, tutelary gods drew up their ranks; the lord of rain washed the air, the lord of wind cleared the dust; chariots uncountable surged like storm surf, riders blackened the plain; long halberds bristled like clouds, plumes swept the rainbow, banners stroked the sky.

sentences.zh: [九]焱焱炎炎，揚光飛文，吐爓生風，吹野燎山，日月為之奪明，丘陵為之搖震。

translations.literal: [Nine] Blazing and fierce they cast light and flying patterns; they breathed flame that raised wind, blew across the fields and kindled the hills; sun and moon lost their light beside them, hills and mounds shook at their tread.
translations.idiomatic: [9] Flames leaped and banners streamed light; breath of fire whipped wind that scoured plain and hill until sun and moon paled and hills trembled under the tread.

sentences.zh: [一０]遂集乎中囿，陳師案屯，駢部曲，列校隊，勒三軍，誓將帥。

translations.literal: [Ten] Then they gathered in the middle preserve, deployed lines and encampments, ranged companies and squads, arrayed the three armies, and the commanders swore the oath.
translations.idiomatic: [10] The hunt closed in the central enclosure: lines and camps were set, companies and files drawn up, the three hosts aligned, and generals bound themselves with oaths.

sentences.zh: [一一]然後舉烽伐鼓，以命三驅，輕車霆發，驍騎電騖，游基發射，范氏施御，弦不失禽，轡不詭遇，飛者未及翔，走者未及去。

translations.literal: [Eleven] Then signal fires were raised and war drums beaten to order the triple drive; light chariots shot like lightning, brave riders raced like thunder; archers on the hunting carts let fly, the Fan clan handled the reins; the bowstring missed no bird, the bridle broke no rule of encounter; flyers had not time to wheel, runners had not time to flee.
translations.idiomatic: [11] Beacons flared, drums beat the three drives; light cars leapt like lightning, picked riders flew like thunder; hunting archers loosed, Fan-style drivers held the lines; no shaft missed its mark, no chase broke the ritual rule; birds could not bank, beasts could not bolt.

sentences.zh: [一二]指顧倏忽，獲車已實，樂不極般，殺不盡物，馬踠余足，士怒未洩，先驅復路，屬車案節。

translations.literal: [Twelve] In a glance, in a flash, the bag-gales were already full; pleasure did not reach excess, slaughter did not exhaust the creatures; horses pawed with strength left, warriors' wrath not yet spent; the vanguard turned home, the escort chariots checked their pace.
translations.idiomatic: [12] In an eyeblink the game-carts sagged full; sport stopped short of excess, the kill never wiped out the herds; horses still stamped with unused fire, soldiers steamed with unspent zeal; the van turned for the capital, the escort checked its pace.

sentences.zh: [一三]於是薦三犧，效五牲，禮神祇，懷百靈，*

translations.literal: [Thirteen] Then they presented the three sacrificial victims, displayed the five livestock, honored the spirits, embraced the hundred souls,
translations.idiomatic: [13] Then came the triple victims, the five kinds of livestock, offerings to the gods, welcome to the hundred spirits,

translations.literal: (yu)
translations.idiomatic: (imperial audience)

sentences.zh: **[覲]*明堂，臨辟雍，揚緝熙，宣皇風，登靈台，考休征。

translations.literal: **audience** at the Bright Hall, approached the Biyong, spread the gentle radiance, proclaimed the imperial wind, ascended the Spirit Terrace and examined auspicious signs.
translations.idiomatic: **paid court** at the Bright Hall, entered the Ring Hall, diffused a mild radiance, proclaimed the royal example, climbed the Spirit Terrace, and read the good omens.

sentences.zh: [一四]俯仰乎乾坤，參象乎聖躬，目中夏而布德，瞰四裔而抗稜。

translations.literal: [Fourteen] Bending and lifting with Heaven and Earth, taking pattern from the sacred person, looking down on the Hua domain to spread virtue, gazing on the four outer tribes to raise the frontier awe.
translations.idiomatic: [14] Rising and bowing with heaven and earth, mirroring the sacred body, he looked down on the central states to spread virtue and out toward the four wilds to overawe the borders.

sentences.zh: [一五]西蕩河源，東澹海漘，北動幽崖，南趯朱垠。

translations.literal: [Fifteen] West he washed the river's source, east he calmed the sea's shore, north he stirred the gloomy cliffs, south he leapt the vermilion margin.
translations.idiomatic: [15] Westward he cleared the river's source, eastward he stilled the sea strand, northward he stirred the shadowed cliffs, southward he sprang the vermilion frontier.

sentences.zh: [一六]殊方別區，界絕而不鄰，自孝武所不能征，孝宣所不能臣，莫不陸讋水栗，奔走而來賓。

translations.literal: [Sixteen] Strange regions and separate zones, bounded off and not adjacent — places Emperor Wu could not conquer and Emperor Xuan could not subject — all quaked on land and trembled on water, ran to attend as guests.
translations.idiomatic: [16] Remote countries, cut off by deserts and seas — lands Emperor Wu never forced and Emperor Xuan never enrolled — now cowered on shore and river and raced to court as guests.

sentences.zh: [一七]遂綏哀牢，開永昌，[一八]春王三朝，會同漢京。

translations.literal: [Seventeen] Then he pacified Ailao, opened Yongchang, [Eighteen] in spring the king's triple court, assembled at the Han capital.
translations.idiomatic: [17] He soothed Ailao and opened Yongchang; [18] in spring, at the triple audience, they gathered at the Han capital.

sentences.zh: 是日也，天子受四海之圖籍，膺萬國之貢珍，內撫諸夏，外接百蠻。

translations.literal: On that day the Son of Heaven received registers from within the four seas and bore tribute treasures from the myriad states; within he settled the Hua tribes, without he received the hundred Man.
translations.idiomatic: That day the Son of Heaven took the maps and registers of the four seas and shouldered tribute from ten thousand domains; within he comforted the Hua peoples, without he drew in the hundred barbarians.

sentences.zh: [一九]乃盛禮樂供帳，置乎雲龍之庭，陳百僚而贊腢後，究皇儀而展帝容。

translations.literal: [Nineteen] Then the grand music and tufted hangings were piled in the court of Cloud and Dragon; the hundred officials were arrayed to praise the masses, and imperial ceremony was searched out to unfold the emperor's mien.
translations.idiomatic: [19] Full ritual music and canopied banners filled the Cloud-and-Dragon court; the hundred bureaus stood ranked to hymn the multitude, and every nuance of imperial demeanor was rehearsed.

sentences.zh: [二０]於是庭實千品，旨酒萬鐘，列金罍，班玉觴，嘉珍御，大牢饗。

translations.literal: [Twenty] Then the court held a thousand kinds of tribute goods, ten thousand goblets of fine wine; golden vessels were ranged, jade cups set out; rare delicacies served, the great sacrifice feasted.
translations.idiomatic: [20] A thousand tribute pieces lined the yard, ten thousand cups of spiced wine; golden jars glittered, jade goblets circled; rare viands steamed, the great ox-feast rose.

sentences.zh: [二一]爾乃食舉雍徹，太師秦樂，陳金石，布絲竹，鐘鼓鏗鎗，管弦曄煜。

translations.literal: [Twenty-one] Then when the meal was raised and Yong played at the clearing, the Grand Music Master presented music; metal and stone were set forth, strings and bamboo spread; bells and drums clashed, pipes and strings gleamed.
translations.idiomatic: [21] When the courses rose and the Yong hymn cleared the tables, the Grand Director of Music led the band; bronze and stone rang, silk and bamboo swelled; bells and drums crashed together, flutes and zithers shone.

sentences.zh: [二二]抗五聲，極六律，歌九功，舞八佾，韶武備，太古畢。

translations.literal: [Twenty-two] They balanced the five tones, exhausted the six pitch-pipes, sang the nine achievements, danced the eight rows; Shao and Wu were complete, utmost antiquity was done.
translations.idiomatic: [22] They tuned the five notes, ran the full six pipes, sang the nine merits, danced the eight files; the music of Shao and Wu stood complete, and the oldest rites were fulfilled.

sentences.zh: [二三]四夷閒奏，德廣所及，仱樹兜離，罔不具集。

translations.literal: [Twenty-three] The four barbarians played in turn; wherever virtue reached, qian shu dou li — none failed to gather.
translations.idiomatic: [23] Barbarian musicians from the four quarters took turns; wherever virtue had reached, every outland air was heard — none stayed away.

sentences.zh: [二四]萬樂備，百禮暨，皇歡浹，腢臣醉，降鞭熅，調元氣，然後撞鐘告罷，百僚遂退。

translations.literal: [Twenty-four] When every tune was ready and every rite performed, the emperor's joy penetrated all, ministers of the masses grew drunk, scourges were lowered and the primal vapor tuned; then they struck the bell to dismiss court and the host of officials withdrew.
translations.idiomatic: [24] When every piece had sounded and every rite closed, imperial delight soaked the hall, the host of ministers reeled; scourges were put away and the primal breath harmonized; then the great bell tolled dismissal and the officials retired.

translations.literal: [Twenty-five]
translations.idiomatic: Twenty-five: section marker in the source.

translations.literal: Note [one]: Xi means light.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [1]: xi means radiance.

translations.literal: Qia means pervading.
translations.idiomatic: Qia means thorough saturation.

sentences.zh: 三雍謂明堂、辟雍、靈台也。

translations.literal: The Three Halls mean the Bright Hall, Biyong, and Spirit Terrace.
translations.idiomatic: The "Three Halls" are the Bright Hall, the Ring Hall, and the Spirit Terrace.

sentences.zh: 永平二年正月，宗祀光武皇帝於明堂，禮畢，登靈台。

translations.literal: In Yongping 2, first month, he performed the lineage sacrifice to Emperor Guangwu in the Bright Hall; when the rites were done he ascended the Spirit Terrace.
translations.idiomatic: In the first month of Yongping 2 he sacrificed to Emperor Guangwu in the Bright Hall, then mounted the Spirit Terrace when the rites ended.

translations.literal: In the third month he approached the Biyong and performed the great archery rites.
translations.idiomatic: In the third month he entered the Biyong for the great archery ceremony.

sentences.zh: 周禮：「王之吉服，享先王即袞冕。

translations.literal: Zhou li: "The king's auspicious robe for sacrificing to former kings is the gun and mian.
translations.idiomatic: The Zhou li states: "For auspicious sacrifice to former kings the king wears the coiled-dragon robe and the crown."

translations.literal: " Zheng Xuan's commentary says: "Gun is the robe with coiled dragons.
translations.idiomatic: " Zheng Xuan explains that gun is the robe embroidered with coiled dragons."

sentences.zh: 」永平二年，帝及公卿列侯始服*

translations.literal: " In Yongping 2 the emperor and dukes, ministers, and ranked nobles for the first time wore
translations.idiomatic: " In Yongping 2 the sovereign together with dukes, ministers, and enfeoffed nobles first donned

translations.literal: (mian)
translations.idiomatic: (the crown)

translations.literal: *caps*[crowns]* and robes.
translations.idiomatic: the prescribed caps, crowns, and robes.

translations.literal: Fu means to spread.
translations.idiomatic: Fu means to publish or spread abroad.

translations.literal: Hong means great.
translations.idiomatic: Hong means vast or great.

translations.literal: Zao means literary pattern.
translations.idiomatic: Zao means literary polish or pattern.

sentences.zh: 謂明堂禮畢，登靈台之後，布詔於天下曰：「建明堂，立辟雍，起靈台，恢弘大道，被之八極。

translations.literal: It refers to after the Bright Hall rites, upon ascending the Spirit Terrace, issuing an edict to the realm saying: "We have raised the Bright Hall, established the Biyong, built the Spirit Terrace, magnified the great Way, and extended it to the eight extremities.
translations.idiomatic: This points to the edict issued after the Bright Hall service and the ascent of the Spirit Terrace: "We have raised the Bright Hall, founded the Ring Hall, built the Spirit Terrace, widened the great Way, and carried it to the eight limits."

translations.literal: " This is "spreading the great literary edict."
translations.idiomatic: " That is what is meant by "spreading the great rescript."

translations.literal: Xin is read as shen (extend).
translations.idiomatic: The graph read xin should be read as shen, "to extend."

translations.literal: Jing means great.
translations.idiomatic: Jing means great or august.

translations.literal: Shuo means beautiful.
translations.idiomatic: Shuo means splendid or beautiful.

sentences.zh: 揚代廟謂上尊號光武廟曰代祖。

translations.literal: Yang dai miao means honoring Guangwu's temple with the title "Substitute Ancestor."
translations.idiomatic: "Raising the substitute shrine" refers to giving Guangwu's temple the honorific title Daizu, the "stand-in" imperial ancestor.

sentences.zh: 正予樂謂依讖文改大樂為大予樂也。

translations.literal: Zheng yu yue means following the prognostication text, changing Da Yue to Da Yu Yue.
translations.idiomatic: "Setting right the Yu music" means following a prophecy to rename the court music Da Yu Yue.

translations.literal: Note [two]: Da lu is the jade chariot.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [2]: the great road means the jade carriage.

translations.literal: Huang qu are the imperial highways.
translations.idiomatic: Huang qu are the ruler's processional ways.

sentences.zh: 易曰：「先王以省方觀人設教。

translations.literal: The Changes says: "The former kings inspected the regions to observe the people and set up teaching.
translations.idiomatic: The Changes says: "The kings of old toured the quarters to watch the people and establish instruction."

translations.literal: " The Documents says: "In the second month of the year he toured eastward.
translations.idiomatic: " The Documents record the eastern tour in the second month."

translations.literal: " It also says: "From north to south sound and teaching reached everywhere.
translations.idiomatic: " They add that from north to south his voice and teaching spread."

translations.literal: " Huang means great.
translations.idiomatic: " Here huang means "great."

translations.literal: Zhu means to illumine.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu means to light up or reveal.

sentences.zh: 注[三]周成王都洛邑，漢又增而修之，故曰增焉。

translations.literal: Note [three]: King Cheng of Zhou made his capital at Luoyang, and Han enlarged and repaired it; hence "enlarged."
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [3]: Zhou's King Cheng had already built at Luoyang; Han added to his work, hence "enlarged."

sentences.zh: 翩翩巍巍，顯顯翼翼，並宮闕顯盛之貌。

translations.literal: Pianpian weiei, xianxian yiyi — all describe the towering splendor of palace and gate.
translations.idiomatic: The binomes describe the soaring, radiant mass of towers and gates.

translations.literal: The Analects says: "Better than the Hua states losing the rites.
translations.idiomatic: The Analects says: "Better that the central states lose nothing essential."

sentences.zh: 」詩商頌曰：「商邑翼翼，四方之極。

translations.literal: " The "Shang hymn" of the Odes says: "The Shang capital stood orderly — the pivot of the four quarters.
translations.idiomatic: " The Shang song in the Odes says: "The Shang city rose in good order — the hub of the four directions."

translations.literal: " Ji means center.
translations.idiomatic: " Ji means the middle or axis."

translations.literal: Luoyang is the center of the land.
translations.idiomatic: Luoyang stands at the heart of the realm.

translations.literal: Note [four]: It means extravagance and frugality accord with ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [4]: both splendor and restraint stay within what ritual allows.

translations.literal: Note [five]: Ping and zao are both water plants.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [5]: duckweed and algae are both aquatic plants.

translations.literal: The Lesser Ya of the Odes says: "The fish are among the algae.
translations.idiomatic: The Shi jing, Lesser Ya, says: "The fish linger among the weeds."

translations.literal: " The Han version of the Odes says: "East lie the park grasses; I yoke my horses and announce the hunt.
translations.idiomatic: " The Han text of the Odes adds: "Eastward lie the meadow grasses; I harness the team and say we go hunting."

sentences.zh: 」薛君傳曰：「圃，博也，有博大之茂草也。

translations.literal: " Xue commentary says: "Pu means broad; there are broad, great lush grasses.
translations.idiomatic: " Xue gloss: pu means extensive — wide stretches of rich pasture."

translations.literal: " Yu also means to rear.
translations.idiomatic: " Yu here means the same as rear or raise."

sentences.zh: 魯詩傳曰：「古有梁鄒者，天子之田也。

translations.literal: The Lu commentary on the Odes says: "In antiquity there was Liang Zou — the Son of Heaven hunting park.
translations.idiomatic: The Lu school says: "Ancient Liang Zou was the royal hunting ground."

sentences.zh: 」詩大雅曰：「王在靈囿，麀鹿攸伏。

translations.literal: " The Greater Ya of the Odes says: "The king was in the spiritual preserve; the does and deer lay there.
translations.idiomatic: " The Greater Ya says: "The king walked his spirit park; hinds and bucks rested there."

sentences.zh: 」毛萇注云：「囿所以域養禽獸也。

translations.literal: " Mao Chang commentary says: "A preserve is what encloses and raises birds and beasts.
translations.idiomatic: " Mao Chang explains that a you is an enclosure for breeding game."

sentences.zh: 」此言魚獸各得其所，如文王之靈囿也。

translations.literal: " This means fish and beasts each had their place, as in King Wen spiritual preserve.
translations.idiomatic: " So fish and game each had its habitat, as under King Wen sacred park."

sentences.zh: 注[六]左傳臧僖伯曰：「春搜夏苗，秋獮冬狩，皆於農隙以講事也。

translations.literal: Note [six]: Zang Xi Bo in the Zuo Tradition says: "Spring sou, summer miao, autumn xian, winter shou — all in the gaps of farming to drill affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [6]: Zang Xi Bo in the Zuo zhuan lists spring hunt, summer review, autumn strike, winter great hunt — each in the slack season to train the host."

translations.literal: " Du Yu commentary says: "Each follows the leisure proper to its season.
translations.idiomatic: " Du Yu notes that each hunt fits the interval left by the farming year."

sentences.zh: 」禮記王制曰「天子諸侯，無事則歲三田。

translations.literal: " The Wang zhi chapter of the Record of Rites says: "The Son of Heaven and feudal lords, when without pressing business, hunt three times a year.
translations.idiomatic: " The Li ji Wang zhi says that king and lords hunt thrice yearly when peace allows."

translations.literal: To hunt without ritual is called violence against Heaven creatures."
translations.idiomatic: Hunting outside the rites is called ravaging what Heaven gave life."

sentences.zh: 注[七]詩國風序曰：「騶虞，搜田以時，仁如騶虞。

translations.literal: Note [seven]: The preface to the state airs in the Odes says: "Zou Yu — the hunt in season; benevolent like Zou Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [7]: the Guo feng preface praises Zou Yu as the timely hunt, humane as the beast of that name."

sentences.zh: 」毛萇注曰：「騶虞，義獸，白虎黑文，不食生物。

translations.literal: " Mao Chang commentary says: "Zou Yu is a righteous creature, a white tiger with black markings; it does not eat the living.
translations.idiomatic: " Mao Chang calls Zou Yu a righteous prodigy, a white tiger striped in black that will not devour living prey."

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「四驖，美襄公也，始命有田狩之事。

translations.literal: " It also says: "Four bays — praising Duke Xiang of Qin, for he first received charge for field and hunt.
translations.idiomatic: " It also introduces Four Bay Steeds, celebrating Duke Xiang of Qin for first receiving the mandate to drill hunting."

translations.literal: " Its poem says: "The four bays are very tall.
translations.idiomatic: " The poem runs: "My four bays are huge and strong."

translations.literal: " The commentary on the day says: "Bay means black horse.
translations.idiomatic: " A gloss on the line says bay means a black horse."

translations.literal: Fu means great."
translations.idiomatic: Fu means big or stout."

sentences.zh: 」又小雅序曰：「車攻，宣王復古也，修車馬，備器械，復會諸侯於東都，因田獵而選車徒焉。

translations.literal: " The preface to the Lesser Ya also says: "Chariot Assault — King Xuan restored antiquity; he repaired chariots and horses, prepared tools and weapons, again assembled the feudal lords at the eastern capital, and by hunting selected chariots and foot.
translations.idiomatic: " The Lesser Ya preface says Che gong celebrates King Xuan return to old ways: he refitted chariot and horse, readied arms, met the lords again at the eastern capital, and used the hunt to pick chariots and foot."

translations.literal: " Its poem says: "My chariot is well tempered, my horses are matched.
translations.idiomatic: " The verse says: "Our cars are tight, our teams are even."

translations.literal: " The commentary says: "Gong means solid.
translations.idiomatic: " The gloss says gong means firm or well-tuned."

sentences.zh: 」又吉日詩曰：「田車既好，四牡孔阜。

translations.literal: " The auspicious-day poem also says: "The field chariot is fine, the four stallions are very tall.
translations.idiomatic: " The Ji ri poem adds: "The hunting wagon is trim, the four bays are mighty."

translations.literal: " Emperor Xuan edict says: "The masters of ceremony shall prepare the ritual forms."
translations.idiomatic: " An edict of Emperor Xuan ordered the ritual officers to set out the full protocol."

sentences.zh: 注[八]鯨魚謂刻杵作鯨魚形也。

translations.literal: Note [eight]: Whale fish means carving the striker in the shape of a whale.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [8]: the whale-shaped striker is a carved whale used to sound the bell.

translations.literal: Keng means to strike it; read ku-geng fan.
translations.idiomatic: Keng means to beat; the fanqie is ku-geng.

sentences.zh: 尚書大傳曰：「天子將出則撞黃鐘，右五鐘皆應。

translations.literal: The Great Tradition on the Documents says: "When the Son of Heaven is about to go forth he strikes the yellow bell, and the five bells to the right all respond.
translations.idiomatic: The Shang shu da zhuan says the sovereign, before setting out, strikes the yellow bell and the five bells answer in turn."

sentences.zh: 」薛綜注西京賦云：「海中有大魚名鯨，又有獸名蒲牢。

translations.literal: " Xue Zong commentary on the Western Capital Rhapsody says: "In the sea there is a great fish called whale, and also a creature called Pulao.
translations.idiomatic: " Xue Zong on the Xi jing fu notes a sea monster called whale and another named Pulao."

sentences.zh: 蒲牢素畏鯨魚，鯨魚擊蒲牢，蒲牢輒大鳴呼。

translations.literal: Pulao always feared the whale; when the whale struck Pulao, Pulao would greatly cry out.
translations.idiomatic: Pulao was terrified of the whale; whenever the whale butted him, Pulao roared.

sentences.zh: 凡鐘欲令其聲大者，故作蒲牢於其上，撞鐘者名為鯨魚。

translations.literal: Whenever they wished the bell sound to be great, they therefore made Pulao on top of it, and the bell-striker was named whale.
translations.idiomatic: To make bells boom they cast Pulao on the crown and nicknamed the striker the whale.

translations.literal: The bell had engraved seal script, hence it is called splendid.
translations.idiomatic: Because the bell bore carved inscriptions, it is called the splendid bell.

translations.literal: " The Erya says: "A horse eight feet or more in height is called a dragon.
translations.idiomatic: " The Erya classes horses over eight feet as dragons."

translations.literal: " The Monthly Ordinances: "In spring one drives the green dragon.
translations.idiomatic: " The Yue ling pairs spring with the azure dragon team."

translations.literal: " Each follows the color of the four seasons; hence they are called seasonal.
translations.idiomatic: " Each team matched the seasonal color, hence horses of the hour."

translations.literal: Linglong is the sound.
translations.idiomatic: Linglong describes the tinkling of the bells.

sentences.zh: 蔡邕獨斷曰：「百官小吏曰天官。

translations.literal: Cai Yong Solitary Decisions says: "The hundred officials and petty clerks are called celestial officers.
translations.idiomatic: Cai Yong Du duan calls the lesser bureaus celestial officers."

translations.literal: " Jin also means abundant.
translations.idiomatic: " Jin here also means full or dense."

translations.literal: Note [nine]: Shan ling means the mountain spirit.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [9]: shan ling is the genius of the mountain.

translations.literal: Zhu means connect; read like zhu (candle).
translations.idiomatic: Zhu means to link; it rhymes like the word for candle.

translations.literal: Fang means the four directions.
translations.idiomatic: Fang means the four quarters.

translations.literal: Yu shi is the Bi constellation.
translations.idiomatic: The rain master is identified with the Bi star.

translations.literal: Feng bo is the Ji constellation.
translations.idiomatic: The wind lord is identified with the Ji star.

sentences.zh: 韓子師曠謂晉平公曰：「黃帝合鬼神於太山，風伯進掃，雨師灑道。

translations.literal: Han Feizi: Shi Kuang said to Duke Ping of Jin: "The Yellow Emperor assembled spirits on Mount Tai; the wind lord came forward to sweep, the rain master sprinkled the road.
translations.idiomatic: Han Feizi records Shi Kuang telling Duke Ping of Jin how the Yellow Thearch met the gods on Tai Mountain while wind swept and rain washed the way."

sentences.zh: 」蔡邕獨斷曰：「天子大駕，備千乘萬騎。

translations.literal: " Cai Yong Solitary Decisions says: "For the great chariot escort of the Son of Heaven, a thousand chariots and ten thousand riders are prepared.
translations.idiomatic: " Cai Yong says the great progress fields a thousand cars and ten thousand riders."

translations.literal: " Yuan rong is the war chariot.
translations.idiomatic: " Yuan rong is the heavy war wagon."

sentences.zh: 詩小雅曰：「元戎十乘，以先啟行。

translations.literal: The Lesser Ya says: "Ten great war chariots go first to open the march.
translations.idiomatic: The Lesser Ya runs: "Ten great cars lead the column."

translations.literal: " Mao Chang commentary says: "Yuan means great.
translations.idiomatic: " Mao Chang glosses yuan as great."

translations.literal: The Xia house called them hook chariots — correctness first;
translations.idiomatic: The Xia called them hook cars — order came first;

translations.literal: the Yin called them yin chariots — speed first;
translations.idiomatic: the Shang called them swift cars — speed came first;

translations.literal: the Zhou called them yuan rong — excellence first."
translations.idiomatic: the Zhou called them yuan rong — quality came first."

translations.literal: The Shuowen says: "Chan is a small spear.
translations.idiomatic: The Shuowen defines chan as a light lance."

translations.literal: " Read shi-yan fan.
translations.idiomatic: " Fanqie: shi-yan."

translations.literal: Hui means sweep; read si-rui fan.
translations.idiomatic: Hui means to sweep; fanqie si-rui.

translations.literal: Note [ten]: Yanyan, yanyan — both describe light.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [10]: the paired binomes describe leaping flame.

translations.literal: Together they are the gleam of halberd, spear, chariot, and horse.
translations.idiomatic: Together they picture the flash of arms, cars, and mounts.

translations.literal: The Shuowen says: "Yan is the flower of fire."
translations.idiomatic: The Shuowen defines yan as the blaze corolla.

translations.literal: Read yi-shan fan.
translations.idiomatic: Read with the fanqie yi-shan.

translations.literal: Zhen is read like zhen (true).
translations.idiomatic: Zhen in the text should be read like zhen, true, for the rhyme.

translations.literal: Note [eleven]: Zhong you means the middle of the preserve.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [11]: middle preserve is the enclosure center.

sentences.zh: 續漢志曰：「大將軍營五部，部校尉一人。

translations.literal: The Later Han Treatise says: "The great general camp has five divisions; each division has one colonel.
translations.idiomatic: The Xu Han zhi says the commander-in-chief army has five divisions, each with a colonel."

sentences.zh: 部下有曲，曲下有屯長一人。

translations.literal: Below the division are companies; below the company is one sergeant of the platoon.
translations.idiomatic: Under each division stand companies, and under each company a platoon leader.

translations.literal: " Pian means to array in rows.
translations.idiomatic: " Pian means to draw up in line."

sentences.zh: 杜預注左傳曰：「百人為隊。

translations.literal: " Du Yu commentary on the Zuo Tradition says: "A hundred men make a dui (company).
translations.idiomatic: " Du Yu on the Zuo zhuan sets a company at one hundred men."

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄周禮注云：「天子六軍，三居一偏。

translations.literal: " Zheng Xuan commentary on the Zhou li says: "The Son of Heaven has six armies; three occupy one wing.
translations.idiomatic: " Zheng Xuan on the Zhou li says the king fields six hosts, three to a wing."

translations.literal: " Hence this speaks of marshaling the three armies.
translations.idiomatic: " Hence the text speaks of binding the three hosts."

sentences.zh: 周禮曰：「腢吏聽誓於前，斬牲以徇陳，曰不用命者斬之。

translations.literal: The Zhou li says: "The host of officials hear the oath in front; they behead a victim and parade the line, saying, Whoever disobeys the command shall be beheaded.
translations.idiomatic: The Zhou li says officers hear the oath before the ranks, kill a victim, and cry that defiance means death."

translations.literal: " Zheng Xuan commentary says: "Host of officials means the commanders."
translations.idiomatic: " Zheng Xuan explains host of officials as the generals."

sentences.zh: 注[一二]谷梁傳曰：「三驅之禮，一為干豆，二為賓客，三為充君之庖。

translations.literal: Note [twelve]: The Guliang Commentary says: "In the rite of the triple drive, the first is for dried meat, the second for guests, the third to fill the ruler kitchen.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [12]: the Guliang zhuan divides the triple drive among ritual dried stores, guest gifts, and the royal kitchen."

translations.literal: " Ting ji and dian wu both mean swift.
translations.idiomatic: " Ting ji and dian wu both mean lightning fast."

translations.literal: You Ji is Yang Youji.
translations.idiomatic: You Ji is the archer Yang Youji.

sentences.zh: 淮南子曰：「楚有神白譸，王自射之，則*

translations.literal: The Huainanzi says: "Chu had a divine white ape; the king shot at it himself, then *
translations.idiomatic: The Huainanzi tells of Chu sacred white ape that the king tried to shoot, then

translations.literal: (hui)
translations.idiomatic: (brandish)

sentences.zh: **[搏]*而嬉，使養由基射之，始調弓矯矢，未發而譸擁木號矣。

translations.literal: **fought* it in sport; he made Yang Youji shoot it. Youji had only tuned the bow and straightened the arrow, not yet released, when the ape hugged a tree and screamed.
translations.idiomatic: sparred with it for sport and set Yang Youji to shoot; Youji had barely nocked the shaft when the ape clung to a tree and howled.

translations.literal: " Fan was the charioteer of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: " Fan was a driver in the service of Zhao."

sentences.zh: 孟子曰：「趙簡子使王良御，終日不獲一禽，反曰：『天下賤工也。

translations.literal: " Mencius says: "Lord Jian of Zhao made Wang Liang drive for him; all day they took not one bird. Returning he said: He is the basest craftsman in the realm.
translations.idiomatic: " Mencius says Zhao Jianzi hired Wang Liang; they bagged nothing all day, and Jianzi called him the worst driver under heaven."

sentences.zh: 』王良曰：『吾為范氏驅馳，終日不獲一，為之詭遇，一朝而獲十。

translations.literal: " Wang Liang said: "When I drove for Fan in the prescribed way, all day we took not one; when I drove a crooked chase, in one morning we took ten.
translations.idiomatic: " Wang Liang answered: By the book I caught nothing; when I cheated the chase we took ten before noon."

sentences.zh: 』」趙岐注曰：「范，法也，為法度之御，應禮之射，終日不得一。

translations.literal: " Zhao Qi commentary says: "Fan means model; driving by the model and shooting by ritual, all day he got not one.
translations.idiomatic: " Zhao Qi explains Fan as by the rule — lawful driving and ritual archery yield nothing in a day."

sentences.zh: 詭遇，非禮射也，則能獲十。

translations.literal: A crooked chase is not ritual archery, yet then he could get ten.
translations.idiomatic: A crooked chase breaks the rites yet fills the bag.

translations.literal: " The bowstring misses no bird refers to Youji.
translations.idiomatic: " The string misses no bird points to Yang Youji."

translations.literal: " The bridle breaks no crooked chase refers to Fan.
translations.idiomatic: " The rein allows no crooked chase points to the Fan school."

sentences.zh: 注[一三]高唐賦曰：「舉功先得，獲車已實。

translations.literal: Note [thirteen]: The Gaotang Rhapsody says: "They raised the tally of merit and first took prey; the bag-gales were already full.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [13]: the Gaotang fu line celebrates carts already heavy with game."

translations.literal: " The Erya says: "Ban means joy.
translations.idiomatic: " The Erya glosses ban as pleasure."

translations.literal: " The Record of Rites says: "Pleasure must not reach excess.
translations.idiomatic: " The Li ji warns that delight must not go to extremes."

translations.literal: " Wan means to bend.
translations.idiomatic: " Wan means to curl or fold under."

translations.literal: The Fangyan says: "Xie means rest.
translations.idiomatic: The Fang yan says xie means to slacken."

translations.literal: " Han Official Regulations: "For the great progress, the escort chariots number eighty-one.
translations.idiomatic: " Han guan yi counts eighty-one cars in the great escort."

translations.literal: " The Sir Vacuous Rhapsody says: "They checked the pace, not yet slackening.
translations.idiomatic: " The Zi xu fu speaks of checking the horses gait."

translations.literal: " It means halting the pace and going slowly.
translations.idiomatic: " That is, easing the horses to a slow walk."

sentences.zh: 注[一四]左傳鄭子太叔曰：「為五牲三犧。

translations.literal: Note [fourteen]: Zi Taishu speech in the Zuo Tradition says: "We prepare the five livestock and three sacrificial victims.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [14]: Zi Taishu in the Zuo zhuan lists five kinds of beast and three victim grades."

sentences.zh: 」杜預注云：「五牲，麋、鹿、志、狼、兔也。

translations.literal: " Du Yu commentary says: "The five livestock are elk, deer, pig, wolf, and hare.
translations.idiomatic: " Du Yu names elk, deer, pig, wolf, and hare as the five kinds."

sentences.zh: 三犧，祭天地宗廟之犧也。

translations.literal: The three victims are the victims for sacrificing to Heaven, earth, and the ancestral temple.
translations.idiomatic: The three victim grades serve Heaven, earth, and the lineage shrines.

translations.literal: " Jiao means sacrifice to Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: " Jiao is the suburban sacrifice to Heaven."

translations.literal: Spirits of Heaven are called shen; spirits of earth are called qi.
translations.idiomatic: Celestial powers are shen; chthonic powers are qi.

translations.literal: The hundred ling are the hundred spirits.
translations.idiomatic: The hundred ling are the host of minor deities.

translations.literal: The Odes says: "Cherish and gentle the hundred spirits.
translations.idiomatic: The Odes bid the king to soothe the hundred spirits."

translations.literal: " Jin means court audience.
translations.idiomatic: " Jin means to hold court."

translations.literal: It means holding audience with the feudal lords in the Bright Hall.
translations.idiomatic: That is, receiving the lords in the Bright Hall.

sentences.zh: 詩大雅曰：「維清緝熙，文王之典。

translations.literal: The Greater Ya of the Odes says: "Only in pure radiance lies King Wen norm.
translations.idiomatic: The Greater Ya says: "In clear and lasting light stands King Wen pattern."

translations.literal: " Zheng Xuan commentary says: "Ji xi means bright light."
translations.idiomatic: " Zheng Xuan glosses ji xi as shining clarity."

translations.literal: " The Documents says: "Auspicious signs.
translations.idiomatic: " The Shang shu speaks of favorable omens."

translations.literal: " Kong Anguo commentary says: "Good — verification of beautiful conduct."
translations.idiomatic: " Kong Anguo glosses them as the proof of virtuous government."

sentences.zh: 注[一五]易系辭曰：「仰則觀象於天，俯則觀法於地，近取諸身，遠取諸物。

translations.literal: Note [fifteen]: The Changes, "Appended Remarks" says: "Looking up he observed the images in Heaven; looking down he observed the models on earth; near at hand he took them from his own person; far off he took them from things.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [15]: the Zhou yi says the sage read patterns above in heaven and below on earth, in the body close at hand and in things far away."

translations.literal: " Sheng gong means the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: " Sheng gong is the living person of the sovereign."

translations.literal: Zhong Xia means the central states.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Xia is another name for China proper.

translations.literal: Kan is read ku-zan fan.
translations.idiomatic: Kan is glossed with the fanqie ku-zan.

translations.literal: Si yi means the four Yi tribes.
translations.idiomatic: The four yi are the outer barbarians of the four quarters.

translations.literal: Leng means awe.
translations.idiomatic: Leng here means majesty that overawes.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰「德以柔中國，刑以威四夷」也。

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition says: "With virtue one softens the central states; with punishments one awes the four Yi."
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo zhuan says virtue mollifies the Hua heartlands while law overawes the four barbarians."

translations.literal: Note [sixteen]: Dang means to wash away.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [16]: dang means to scour or cleanse.

translations.literal: The river source lies on Mount Kunlun.
translations.idiomatic: The Yellow River rises on Kunlun.

translations.literal: The Former Documents says: "His might and awe stirred the neighboring states.
translations.idiomatic: The Han shu says his majesty rippled through neighboring kingdoms."

translations.literal: " The Yin-yi says: "Dan means to move; read tu-lan fan.
translations.idiomatic: " Sound glosses take dan as stirring, fanqie tu-lan."

translations.literal: " Chun is the water margin; read like chun (lip).
translations.idiomatic: " Chun is the bank; read like the word for lip."

sentences.zh: 郭璞注爾雅曰：「涯上平坦而下水深者為漘。

translations.literal: Guo Pu commentary on the Erya says: "Where the bank is level above and the water deep below is called chun.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Pu on the Erya defines a strand that shelves shallow above and drops deep below."

translations.literal: " Yue means leap; read ta-li fan.
translations.idiomatic: " Yue means to spring; fanqie ta-li."

translations.literal: The Shuowen says: "Yin means boundary.
translations.idiomatic: The Shuowen defines yin as a border mark."

translations.literal: " Read yin.
translations.idiomatic: " Read with the yin sound."

translations.literal: Note [seventeen]: The Erya says: "Zhe means fear.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [17]: the Erya glosses zhe as dread."

translations.literal: " Read zhi-she fan.
translations.idiomatic: " Fanqie zhi-she."

translations.literal: Note [eighteen]: Sui means to pacify.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary [18]: sui means to settle or reassure.

translations.literal: Ailao is the name of a southwestern Yi tribe.
translations.idiomatic: Ailao was a people of the southwest.
